# L26 — Common Implementation Gaps and How to Fix Them

**Module**: M10 — Code Efficacy Assessment  
**Type**: Applied  
**Estimated time**: 25 minutes

---

## Why Gaps Happen

Gaps are not caused by ignorance — they are caused by inattention, time pressure, or subtle misunderstanding of a rule's scope. This lesson catalogs the most common gaps found in Meridian implementations, with the exact symptoms, root cause, and fix for each.

---

## Gap 1: Missing `workspace_id` in a DB Query

**Symptom**: A query returns more rows than expected, or a user can access another workspace's data.

**Root cause**: Developer wrote the query thinking RLS alone is sufficient — RLS is a safety net, not a substitute for explicit scoping.

**Incorrect code**:
```typescript
// ❌ Trusts RLS to scope results — no explicit workspace filter
const page = await db.query.pages.findFirst({
  where: eq(pages.id, pageId)
})
```

**Fix**:
```typescript
// ✅ Explicit AND-scoped with workspace_id
const page = await db.query.pages.findFirst({
  where: and(
    eq(pages.workspace_id, workspaceId),
    eq(pages.id, pageId)
  )
})
```

**Rule**: Explicit `workspace_id` filter is mandatory even when RLS is active. RLS is defense-in-depth, not primary isolation.

---

## Gap 2: Zod Schema Applied After DB Query

**Symptom**: Type errors at runtime, malformed data in the database, or inconsistent API behavior.

**Root cause**: Developer placed Zod validation after some early handler logic (e.g., header checks), inadvertently allowing unvalidated data to flow to the DB.

**Incorrect code**:
```typescript
// ❌ Queries DB before validating the request body
router.post('/blocks', async (req, res, next) => {
  const page = await db.select()...  // using req.body.pageId — unvalidated
  const parsed = createBlockSchema.safeParse(req.body)
  if (!parsed.success) { next(parsed.error); return }
  // ...
})
```

**Fix**:
```typescript
// ✅ Validate FIRST, then use parsed.data in all downstream calls
router.post('/blocks', async (req, res, next) => {
  const parsed = createBlockSchema.safeParse(req.body)
  if (!parsed.success) { next(parsed.error); return }
  
  const page = await db.select()...where(eq(pages.id, parsed.data.pageId))
  // ...
})
```

---

## Gap 3: Audit Log Skipped for AI Actions

**Symptom**: `audit_log` table has no entries, or has entries for some AI endpoints but not others.

**Root cause**: Developer added `logAuditEvent` to the first AI endpoint they built and forgot to add it to subsequent endpoints. Or — audit logging was placed after the provider call and silently skipped when the provider threw an error.

**Incorrect code**:
```typescript
// ❌ Audit log added after provider call — skipped if provider throws
const result = await provider.complete(messages)
await logAuditEvent({ action: 'ai.complete', ... })
```

**Fix**:
```typescript
// ✅ Audit log before provider call — always executes
await logAuditEvent({ action: 'ai.complete', ... })
const result = await provider.complete(messages)
```

---

## Gap 4: bcrypt Cost Factor Lower Than 12

**Symptom**: Passwords are being hashed with cost factor 10 (Node.js default) or lower.

**Root cause**: Developer used `bcrypt.hash(password, 10)` from tutorial code or copied from a previous project. Cost factor 10 is the most common "example" value — it is too low for production.

**Incorrect code**:
```typescript
// ❌ bcrypt default — cost 10 is too low
const SALT_ROUNDS = 10
const hash = await bcrypt.hash(password, SALT_ROUNDS)
```

**Fix**:
```typescript
// ✅ Cost 12 as a named constant
const BCRYPT_ROUNDS = 12
const hash = await bcrypt.hash(password, BCRYPT_ROUNDS)
```

The named constant also makes the value searchable — grep for `BCRYPT_ROUNDS` to verify it across the codebase.

---

## Gap 5: `SET` Instead of `SET LOCAL` in Tenant Middleware

**Symptom**: Workspace context leaks between requests when using connection pooling. One user's `workspace_id` appears in another user's query results.

**Root cause**: Developer used `SET app.current_workspace_id = ...` (session-level) instead of `SET LOCAL ...` (transaction-scoped). In a connection pool, session-level settings persist to the next request that reuses the connection.

**Incorrect code**:
```typescript
// ❌ Session-level SET — persists beyond the request
await db.execute(sql`SET app.current_workspace_id = ${workspaceId}`)
```

**Fix**:
```typescript
// ✅ Transaction-scoped SET LOCAL — reset when transaction ends
await db.execute(sql`SET LOCAL app.current_workspace_id = ${workspaceId}`)
```

This requires that tenant middleware runs inside a transaction context. If using Drizzle without an explicit transaction, wrap the handler in `db.transaction(async (tx) => { ... })`.

---

## Gap 6: User Input Concatenated Into System Prompt

**Symptom**: AI assistant responds to injection attempts embedded in user messages.

**Root cause**: Developer built the system prompt by string interpolation, including user-provided content.

**Incorrect code**:
```typescript
// ❌ User input in the system prompt
const systemPrompt = `
  You are a helpful assistant for ${user.name}.
  The user is currently asking about: ${userInput}
  Help them with their question.
`
```

**Fix**:
```typescript
// ✅ User input only in the messages array, never in systemPrompt
const systemPrompt = `You are a helpful assistant for ${user.name}.`
const messages = [{ role: 'user', content: userInput }]
```

---

## Gap Pattern Summary

| Gap | Symptom | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Missing `workspace_id` | Cross-tenant data leakage | Add `workspace_id` to WHERE clause |
| Zod after handler logic | Unvalidated data in DB | Move `safeParse` to line 1 of handler |
| Audit log after provider call | Missing audit entries on error | Move `logAuditEvent` before provider call |
| bcrypt cost < 12 | Weaker password security | Change to `12`, use named constant |
| `SET` not `SET LOCAL` | Context leaks between requests | Change to `SET LOCAL`, wrap in transaction |
| User input in `systemPrompt` | Injection vulnerability | Move user input to `messages[]` only |

---

## Key Points

- The most common gaps are subtle omissions, not architectural failures — they are easy to introduce under time pressure.
- "RLS is active" does not mean you can omit `workspace_id` from queries. They serve different purposes.
- `SET LOCAL` is not optional when using a connection pool — session-level `SET` will cause context leaks.
- Audit log placement must be before the provider call. After = audit skipped on provider error.
- Never use tutorial-copy bcrypt values (`10`) in production. `12` must be a named constant.
lessons.L27:
# L27 — From Gap Report to Production-Ready Implementation

**Module**: M10 — Code Efficacy Assessment  
**Type**: Applied  
**Estimated time**: 30 minutes

---

## The Goal of This Lesson

You have built Meridian. You have the Five-Question Framework. You know what the common gaps look like. This lesson shows you how to use them together — systematically auditing your own implementation and producing a structured remediation plan that moves you from "working code" to "production-ready code."

---

## Step 1: Run the Five-Question Framework as a Code Audit

For each of the five questions, perform a targeted search across your codebase.

### Audit Script (run from the repo root)

```bash
echo "=== Q1: Queries missing workspace_id ==="
# Look for .where() calls without workspace_id — review each result manually
grep -rn "\.where(eq(" server/src --include="*.ts" | grep -v "workspace_id"

echo ""
echo "=== Q2: Routes missing safeParse ==="
# Look for route handlers without safeParse in the first block
grep -n "router\.\(post\|put\|patch\)" server/src/routes --include="*.ts" -l

echo ""
echo "=== Q3: AI provider calls missing audit log ==="
grep -n "provider\.\(complete\|stream\)" server/src --include="*.ts" -B5 | grep -v "logAuditEvent"

echo ""
echo "=== Q4: bcrypt cost not equal to 12 ==="
grep -rn "bcrypt\.hash(" server/src --include="*.ts"

echo ""
echo "=== Q5: Refresh handler — check for reuse detection ==="
grep -n "rt:" server/src/routes/auth.ts
```

These are grep heuristics — manual review is required for each result. The grep output tells you where to look, not what the verdict is.

---

## Step 2: Produce a Gap Report

For each violation found, write a structured gap entry:

```markdown
## Gap Report — [Date]
Generated by: Five-Question Efficacy Framework

### GAP-001
- **Question**: Q1 — workspace_id in WHERE
- **File**: server/src/routes/pages.ts
- **Line**: 87
- **Description**: `db.select().from(blocks).where(eq(blocks.page_id, pageId))` omits workspace_id
- **Risk**: Cross-tenant block data leakage if RLS context not set
- **Fix**: Add `eq(blocks.workspace_id, req.workspace.id)` to the WHERE clause

### GAP-002
- **Question**: Q3 — Audit log before AI call
- **File**: server/src/routes/ai.ts
- **Line**: 134
- **Description**: `provider.stream(...)` called before `logAuditEvent`
- **Risk**: AI stream actions not logged if provider throws on initialization
- **Fix**: Move `logAuditEvent` call to before `provider.stream(...)`
```

---

## Step 3: Prioritize by Risk Level

Not all gaps are equal. Prioritize by the potential impact if exploited:

| Priority | Gap types |
|---|---|
| P0 — Fix immediately | Missing `workspace_id` (cross-tenant leakage), `SET` not `SET LOCAL` (context bleed) |
| P1 — Fix before first user | Missing audit log, bcrypt cost < 12 |
| P2 — Fix before public launch | Missing Zod validation, user input in system prompt |

P0 gaps must be fixed before the next commit. P1 gaps before any user data enters the system. P2 gaps before external traffic.

---

## Step 4: Apply Fixes in Isolation

Fix one gap at a time. Each fix should be a separate, focused commit:

```
[security] Add workspace_id to blocks query in pages route

Fixes GAP-001. The WHERE clause in GET /pages/:id/blocks was missing
workspace_id, relying solely on RLS for tenant isolation. Explicit
workspace_id filter added per architecture non-negotiable rules.

Closes: GAP-001
```

Focused commits make gap fixes easy to review, revert if needed, and reference in the gap log.

---

## Step 5: Re-run the Framework After Each Fix

After applying a fix, run the same grep query from Step 1 for that specific question. Verify the violation no longer appears. Do not mark a gap as resolved until the re-run confirms it.

---

## Ongoing: The CI Efficacy Check

After your initial audit is clean, add the framework checks to CI:

```yaml
# .github/workflows/efficacy.yml
name: Efficacy Check

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  five-question-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Q4 — Check bcrypt cost factor
        run: |
          # Fail if any bcrypt.hash() call does not use 12
          if grep -rn "bcrypt\.hash(" server/src --include="*.ts" | grep -v ", 12)"; then
            echo "❌ bcrypt cost factor is not 12"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "✅ bcrypt cost factor is 12 everywhere"
      
      - name: Q1 — Check for workspace_id in queries (advisory)
        run: |
          echo "Advisory: Review these WHERE clauses for missing workspace_id:"
          grep -rn "\.where(eq(" server/src --include="*.ts" | grep -v "workspace_id" || true
```

The Q4 check can be fully automated (exact grep pattern). The Q1 check is advisory — grep cannot prove the presence of workspace_id in every Drizzle query pattern, but it flags suspicious results for manual review.

---

## What "Production-Ready" Means in Meridian

A Meridian implementation is production-ready when:

1. All five framework questions return zero violations
2. The audit_log table has an entry for every AI action in the test suite
3. The adversarial RLS test (from L22) passes
4. All bcrypt hashes in the codebase use cost 12
5. The refresh token handler has a reuse detection branch
6. No user-provided text appears in any system prompt string

This is not comprehensive security — it is the minimum bar defined by the architecture's non-negotiable rules. Production security also requires dependency audits, penetration testing, infrastructure hardening, and ongoing monitoring.

---

## Key Points

- The gap audit is systematic, not intuitive — use grep patterns and review every result.
- Gaps are prioritized P0/P1/P2 by blast radius: tenant leakage first, audit/crypto second, input validation third.
- Fix one gap per commit. Small, reversible, reviewable.
- Re-run the specific framework question after each fix to confirm resolution.
- CI efficacy checks automate the verifiable questions (bcrypt cost) and surface advisory results for manual review.
- "Production-ready" is a checklist, not a feeling — every item on the checklist has a verifiable answer.

cards.front: What are the three load-bearing constraints that determine Meridian's entire technology stack?
cards.back:
1. Browser-only collaborative editor (forces Lexical + Yjs CRDT)
2. Below-app-layer tenant isolation (forces PostgreSQL RLS)
3. In-database vector storage (forces pgvector, eliminates external vector DBs)

cards.front: Why is Next.js incompatible with Meridian's architecture?
cards.back: Next.js server-side rendering conflicts with Yjs CRDT, which requires browser-only state. Collaborative editing state cannot be rendered server-side. Vite SPA is architecturally mandatory.

cards.front: What is an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) and what four sections does it contain?
cards.back: An ADR documents a technology choice made in a project. Sections: (1) Context — the constraint or problem, (2) Decision — what was chosen, (3) Rationale — why it eliminates alternatives, (4) Consequences — what the choice commits you to.

cards.front: What is the correct pnpm workspace structure for Meridian?
cards.back: Root package.json with `workspaces: ['client', 'server', 'db']`. Each package has its own package.json. Run `pnpm install` from the root to hoist shared dependencies. TypeScript project references link packages.

cards.front: What TypeScript compiler options are non-negotiable in Meridian's tsconfig?
cards.back: `strict: true`, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes: true`. These enforce TypeScript strict mode across all packages. No `any`, no `as unknown`.

cards.front: In Docker Compose for Meridian, what hostname does the server container use to connect to PostgreSQL?
cards.back: `postgres` — the service name in docker-compose.yml, not `localhost`. The server container and postgres container are on the same Docker network, so `postgres` resolves correctly.

cards.front: What are the four services in Meridian's Docker Compose topology?
cards.back:
1. `postgres` — PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector
2. `redis` — Redis 7 for session storage
3. `server` — Node.js Express API
4. `client` — Vite dev server with proxy

Server depends_on postgres and redis.

cards.front: What primary key type do all Meridian tables use, and what extension provides it?
cards.back: UUID v4 primary keys using `uuid_generate_v4()`. Provided by the `uuid-ossp` extension. Never use auto-increment integers — UUIDs prevent ID enumeration attacks and work across distributed systems.

cards.front: What timestamp type should all Meridian tables use for created_at and updated_at, and why?
cards.back: `TIMESTAMPTZ` (timestamp with time zone). Stores the absolute moment in UTC. Plain `TIMESTAMP` without timezone causes ambiguity when clients are in different timezones.

cards.front: Why does the audit_log table use `ON DELETE SET NULL` for user_id and workspace_id foreign keys?
cards.back: Compliance. Audit records must survive entity deletion. If a user or workspace is deleted, the audit entry is preserved with `user_id = NULL` and `workspace_id = NULL` rather than being cascade-deleted.

cards.front: What SQL command enables Row Level Security on a table?
cards.back:
`ALTER TABLE table_name ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;`

RLS must be enabled per table AND a policy must be created. Enabling without a policy blocks all access by non-owners.

cards.front: What does `AS RESTRICTIVE` mean in an RLS policy, and why is it used in Meridian?
cards.back: `AS RESTRICTIVE` means the policy must be satisfied even when other policies allow access — it cannot be bypassed by any other permissive policy. Meridian uses it to guarantee workspace isolation cannot be overridden.

cards.front: What function sets the RLS workspace context, and what is its third parameter for?
cards.back:
`set_config('app.current_workspace_id', workspaceId, true)`

Third parameter `true` = transaction-local. The setting is scoped to the current transaction and reset automatically when the transaction ends.

cards.front: What database role privilege bypasses all RLS policies, and who should never have it?
cards.back: `BYPASSRLS` privilege bypasses all RLS policies. The application database user (`meridian_app`) must NEVER have this privilege. Verify with: `SELECT rolbypassrls FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname = 'meridian_app'` — must be `f`.

cards.front: What pgvector column type stores embeddings, and what dimension is used for Meridian?
cards.back: `vector(1536)` — the dimension matches the embedding model output (OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002 and compatible models produce 1536-dimensional vectors).

cards.front: What index type does Meridian use for pgvector similarity search, and what operator class?
cards.back:
HNSW index with `vector_cosine_ops` operator class.
```sql
CREATE INDEX ON user_memory USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops);
```
HNSW provides approximate nearest neighbor search with better query performance than IVFFlat.

cards.front: What are the three memory tiers in Meridian's user_memory table?
cards.back:
- `session` — cleared at end of conversation, ephemeral context
- `medium` — persists days to weeks, subject to LRU eviction
- `deep` — permanent, requires explicit promotion signal

Enforced by `CHECK (tier IN ('session', 'medium', 'deep'))`.

cards.front: What bcrypt cost factor does Meridian use for password hashing, and why not lower?
cards.back: `12`. This is the minimum cost defined by the architecture. Lower values (e.g., the Node.js default of `10`) are computationally cheaper to brute-force. Cost 12 adds ~400ms per hash — acceptable for login, expensive for an attacker.

cards.front: What JWT expiry time does Meridian use for access tokens, and what claim carries the user ID?
cards.back: 15 minutes expiry (`expiresIn: '15m'`). The `sub` claim carries the user ID as a string. Short expiry limits the window of exploitation if a token is stolen.

cards.front: Why must login handlers use a dummy bcrypt hash when the user is not found?
cards.back: To prevent timing attacks. If the server returns immediately on 'user not found' but takes 400ms for 'wrong password', an attacker can enumerate valid email addresses by comparing response times. A dummy hash ensures consistent response time.

cards.front: How are refresh tokens stored in Redis, and why are they hashed?
cards.back: Key: `rt:{userId}`, value: SHA-256 hash of the token. Raw tokens are not stored. If Redis is compromised, the attacker gets hashes — not usable tokens. The client stores the raw token; the server only ever has the hash.

cards.front: What does refresh token reuse detection protect against, and how is it implemented?
cards.back: Protects against stolen refresh tokens. When a refresh request arrives and the Redis key is already gone (deleted by a previous rotation), it means the token was already used — either by the legitimate user or an attacker. The server revokes all tokens for the user.

cards.front: What is the correct Meridian middleware pipeline order?
cards.back:
Helmet → CORS → rateLimit → JSON body parser → auth → workspace → Zod → handler → errorHandler

Order matters: security headers before any parsing, auth before workspace, validation before handler logic.

cards.front: How does Express know which middleware handles 4xx validation errors vs 5xx server errors?
cards.back: Both use the four-parameter error handler `(err, req, res, next)`. Inside, check the error type: ZodError → 400, AuthenticationError → 401, AuthorizationError → 403, all others → 500. One handler, type-based branching.

cards.front: What does Zod's `safeParse` return, and why use it instead of `parse`?
cards.back: `safeParse` returns `{ success: true, data }` or `{ success: false, error }`. It never throws. `parse` throws a `ZodError` on failure. In Express handlers, `safeParse` + early return is cleaner than wrapping every validation in try/catch.

cards.front: Where in a route handler must Zod validation occur?
cards.back: As the first action — before any DB queries, before any business logic. Validate, then act on `parsed.data` only. Any DB call using `req.body` directly (before validation) is a gap.

cards.front: What are the four steps of the `requireWorkspace` tenant middleware?
cards.back:
1. Get `workspaceId` from `req.params` (not `req.body`)
2. Verify user is a member of the workspace
3. Call `SET LOCAL app.current_workspace_id = workspaceId`
4. Attach `req.workspace = { id, role }` for use in handlers

cards.front: Why must `workspace_id` come from `req.params` (or verified middleware), never from `req.body`?
cards.back: A client can send any `workspace_id` in `req.body`. Trusting it would allow a user to scope their request to any workspace. `req.params` is controlled by the route pattern; `req.workspace.id` from middleware is verified against the user's memberships.

cards.front: What does Vite's `server.proxy` configuration do in Meridian's dev setup?
cards.back: Proxies API requests from the Vite dev server to the Express server, avoiding CORS issues during development. `/api/*` → `http://server:3000` (Docker network) or `http://localhost:3001` (local). The browser only talks to Vite; Vite forwards to Express.

cards.front: What React 18 rule applies to all state updates, and why does it matter for Lexical?
cards.back: State updates must be immutable — never mutate objects in place. For Lexical: `EditorState` is immutable. Every change produces a new `EditorState`. Access state via `editor.getEditorState().read(() => {...})` — never mutate the existing state.

cards.front: What three states must a `RequireAuth` component handle?
cards.back:
1. `isLoading === true` → render null or spinner (don't redirect while checking)
2. `user !== null` → render `<Outlet />` (authenticated)
3. `user === null` → `<Navigate to='/login' replace />` (not authenticated)

cards.front: What Zustand pattern prevents stale closures when reading store state outside React components?
cards.back: `useAuthStore.getState()` — direct store access that always returns the current state. Contrast with `useAuthStore()` (hook) which is only valid inside React components and subject to closure staleness.

cards.front: Why should a Zustand auth store include an `isLoading` field?
cards.back: On page load, the client needs to check for a valid session (call `/api/auth/me`). During this async check, the auth state is unknown. Without `isLoading`, the UI flashes the login screen before the session check completes. `isLoading: true` during the check prevents premature redirects.

cards.front: What are the three route tiers in Meridian's React Router structure?
cards.back:
1. Public routes — `/login`, `/register` (no auth required)
2. Auth guard — `RequireAuth` wrapper for all authenticated routes
3. Workspace routes — `/workspace/:id/*` with `WorkspaceShell` and `requireWorkspace` middleware on the server

cards.front: What is Lexical's EditorState, and how do you read it without mutations?
cards.back: `EditorState` is an immutable snapshot of all editor content. Read it with: `editor.getEditorState().read(() => { const root = $getRoot(); ... })`. The `read()` callback provides a read-only context — mutations inside it will throw.

cards.front: How do you make a change to Lexical editor content programmatically?
cards.back: `editor.update(() => { /* mutations here */ })` — mutations must be inside an `update()` callback. Lexical creates a new `EditorState` from the mutation. Never mutate nodes outside an `update()` call.

cards.front: What two data representations does Meridian use for page content?
cards.back:
1. **Yjs Y.Doc** — CRDT for real-time collaborative editing state in memory
2. **`blocks` table** — persistent, queryable block model in PostgreSQL

The Yjs doc is the live state; blocks are the durable record. `syncPageBlocks()` reconciles them.

cards.front: What is the `LLMProvider` interface, and why use an abstraction instead of calling Anthropic directly?
cards.back: An interface that all AI providers implement: `{ complete(req): Promise<string>, stream(req): AsyncIterable<string> }`. Using an abstraction allows switching providers (Anthropic → OpenAI → Ollama) without changing route handlers. The `LLMRouter` selects the provider at runtime.

cards.front: What must happen to the `audit_log` table BEFORE calling any LLM provider method?
cards.back: `logAuditEvent()` must be called before `provider.complete()` or `provider.stream()`. If the provider call fails, the audit entry still exists. Logging after the call means failures leave no trace.

cards.front: What HTTP mechanism does Meridian use for AI streaming responses, and how does the server signal the start/end?
What two fields must ALWAYS appear in the WHERE clause of a pgvector similarity search in Meridian?
cards.back: `user_id = $1 AND workspace_id = $2` — both must be present. Omitting either creates a privacy gap: user memories from another user (missing `user_id`) or another workspace (missing `workspace_id`) could be returned.

cards.front: What distance threshold does Meridian use to filter pgvector results, and what does it represent?
cards.back: `distance < 0.3` (cosine distance). Values closer to 0 are more similar. Threshold 0.3 means only memories with ≥70% semantic similarity to the query are included. Higher threshold = more results but lower relevance.

cards.front: What SQL operator performs cosine distance comparison in pgvector?
cards.back: `<=>` — cosine distance operator. Example: `ORDER BY embedding <=> $query_vector LIMIT 10`. Lower values = more similar. Also available: `<->` (L2/Euclidean), `<#>` (inner product).

cards.front: How are session memories cleared in Meridian?
cards.back: Explicit deletion at session end: `db.delete(user_memory).where(and(eq(user_memory.user_id, userId), eq(user_memory.workspace_id, workspaceId), eq(user_memory.tier, 'session')))`. Session memories are not TTL-expired — they are explicitly cleared.

cards.front: What triggers medium memory eviction, and what column determines staleness?
cards.back: `last_accessed_at` — if not accessed for 30 days, the medium memory is evicted. Every read of a memory updates `last_accessed_at`. This is LRU eviction at the tier level.

cards.front: In what order should memory tiers be retrieved for AI context assembly?
cards.back: Session memories first (most current context), then deep memories (permanent facts), then medium memories (recent context). Session context overrides long-term context when relevant.

cards.front: What SQL query verifies RLS is enabled on all workspace-scoped tables?
cards.back:
`SELECT tablename, rowsecurity FROM pg_tables WHERE schemaname = 'public' ORDER BY tablename;`

Expect `rowsecurity = true` for: audit_log, block_versions, blocks, memory_graph, pages, user_memory, workspace_members.

cards.front: What is the adversarial RLS test, and why must it be in the automated test suite?
cards.back: Set context to workspace B, then query workspace A's data — expect 0 rows. If rows are returned, RLS is either disabled or misconfigured. Must be automated because RLS verification cannot be done by reading code alone; it requires runtime database behavior.

cards.front: What two PostgreSQL role attributes must the Meridian app user NEVER have?
cards.back: `rolsuper = f` (not superuser) and `rolbypassrls = f` (cannot bypass RLS). Verify: `SELECT rolsuper, rolbypassrls FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname = 'meridian_app';` — both must be `f`.

cards.front: Why should database migrations be run by a different role than the application role?
cards.back: The app role has SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE only — no CREATE TABLE or schema modification. Migrations require DDL permissions. Mixing DDL and DML in one role violates least privilege. Use a separate migration role (or postgres superuser) for schema changes.

cards.front: What `ON DELETE` behavior does audit_log use for user_id and workspace_id, and why?
cards.back: `ON DELETE SET NULL`. Audit records persist when users or workspaces are deleted. The foreign key becomes null, preserving the historical record. `ON DELETE CASCADE` would destroy the audit trail — never use cascade on audit tables.

cards.front: What should NOT be stored in the audit_log payload field?
cards.back: Message content, user-provided text, embedding vectors, system prompt content. The payload is JSONB and queryable — it must never contain PII. Log metadata only: model name, message count, memory tier, result count.

cards.front: What are the six AI action types that must be logged to audit_log?
cards.back:
1. `ai.complete` — non-streaming completion
2. `ai.stream` — streaming response
3. `ai.memory.write` — memory stored
4. `ai.memory.retrieve` — RAG retrieval
5. `ai.memory.promote` — tier promotion
6. `ai.memory.evict` — memory eviction

cards.front: What is prompt injection, and why can't it be fully prevented?
cards.back: Prompt injection is when user input overrides system instructions by being included in the LLM's instruction context. It cannot be fully prevented because LLMs process all text in context as potential instructions. Defense is structural isolation and impact reduction, not elimination.

cards.front: Where must user input go in the LLM API call, and where must it NEVER go?
cards.back: User input MUST go in `messages[].content` — the structured messages array. It must NEVER be concatenated into the `systemPrompt` string. The API structurally separates these two inputs, limiting injection scope.

cards.front: What two techniques protect against memory injection into the system prompt?
cards.back:
1. **Sanitization** — strip phrases like 'ignore previous instructions', 'you are now', 'act as' from memory values before injection
2. **XML framing** — wrap memory in `<user_context>` tags to signal 'this is data, not instructions'

cards.front: What is Q1 of the Five-Question Efficacy Framework?
cards.back:
Does every workspace-scoped DB query include `workspace_id` in the WHERE clause?

Fail pattern: `.where(eq(table.id, id))` without `eq(table.workspace_id, workspaceId)`. Even with RLS active, explicit scoping is mandatory.

cards.front: What is Q2 of the Five-Question Efficacy Framework?
cards.back:
Is every POST/PUT/PATCH route protected by a Zod schema before any handler logic?

Fail pattern: Any DB call or business logic appearing before `safeParse` in a route handler.

cards.front: What is Q3 of the Five-Question Efficacy Framework?
cards.back:
Is every AI action logged to `audit_log` BEFORE execution?

Fail pattern: `provider.complete(...)` or `provider.stream(...)` called before `logAuditEvent()`.

cards.front: What is Q4 of the Five-Question Efficacy Framework?
cards.back:
Is bcrypt cost factor set to exactly 12?

Fail pattern: `bcrypt.hash(password, 10)` or `bcrypt.hash(password, ROUNDS)` where ROUNDS is not verified to be 12.

cards.front: What is Q5 of the Five-Question Efficacy Framework?
cards.back:
Are refresh tokens rotated on every use with reuse detection?

Fail pattern: Refresh handler that reissues tokens without checking if the Redis key is already gone (reuse signal).

cards.front: What is the most dangerous gap in Meridian implementations and why?
cards.back: Missing `workspace_id` in DB queries — it silently allows cross-tenant data access. RLS may catch it as a safety net, but if the workspace context is not set (e.g., middleware skipped), the query returns data from any tenant.

cards.front: Why does `SET` instead of `SET LOCAL` in tenant middleware cause a critical bug?
cards.back: `SET` is session-level — persists for the lifetime of the connection in a pool. When the next request reuses the pooled connection, it inherits the previous request's `workspace_id`. `SET LOCAL` is transaction-scoped and resets automatically.

cards.front: What gap occurs when audit log is placed after the AI provider call?
cards.back: If the provider throws an error, the `await logAuditEvent()` line is never reached. The AI call occurs with no audit trace. Compliance requirement: every AI action must have a trace, including failed ones.

cards.front: What is a P0 gap in Meridian, and give two examples?
cards.back:
P0 = fix immediately, before next commit. Examples:
1. Missing `workspace_id` in a DB query (cross-tenant data leakage)
2. `SET` not `SET LOCAL` in tenant middleware (workspace context bleeding between requests)

cards.front: What is a P1 gap in Meridian, and give two examples?
cards.back:
P1 = fix before any user data enters the system. Examples:
1. Missing `logAuditEvent` before AI provider call
2. bcrypt cost factor < 12

cards.front: What is a P2 gap in Meridian, and give two examples?
cards.back:
P2 = fix before external/public traffic. Examples:
1. Missing Zod validation on a route (handler uses `req.body` directly)
2. User input concatenated into the AI system prompt

cards.front: How should each gap fix be committed in version control?
cards.back: One gap per commit. Commit message format: `[security] Short description` with body explaining the gap reference (GAP-001), file, and rule violated. Small, focused commits are reversible and reviewable.

cards.front: What does a complete Meridian gap report entry contain?
cards.back: Gap ID, Five-Question number (Q1–Q5), file path, line number, description of the violation, risk if unresolved, and the specific code fix.

cards.front: What six conditions define 'production-ready' for a Meridian implementation?
cards.back:
1. All five framework questions: zero violations
2. `audit_log` has entries for every AI action in test suite
3. Adversarial RLS test passes
4. All bcrypt hashes use cost 12
5. Refresh token handler has reuse detection branch
6. No user-provided text in any system prompt string

cards.front: What is tenant isolation and what two mechanisms enforce it in Meridian?
cards.back: Tenant isolation ensures one workspace cannot access another workspace's data. Enforced by: (1) PostgreSQL RLS with `SET LOCAL app.current_workspace_id` — database-level enforcement, and (2) explicit `workspace_id` in every WHERE clause — application-level enforcement. Defense-in-depth: both required.

cards.front: Why does Meridian use Redis for refresh token storage rather than PostgreSQL?
cards.back: Redis TTL natively handles token expiry without cron jobs. Redis key deletion is O(1) — constant time. Refresh token operations are high-frequency and latency-sensitive. PostgreSQL is for durable relational data; Redis is for ephemeral session state.

cards.front: What is the difference between SSE and WebSocket, and when does Meridian use each?
cards.back:
SSE = server-to-client only, unidirectional, HTTP, simple reconnect logic. WebSocket = bidirectional, persistent connection, custom protocol.

Meridian uses SSE for AI streaming (server pushes text to client) and WebSocket (via ws library) for Yjs collaborative editing (bidirectional document sync).

cards.front: What does `X-Accel-Buffering: no` do in SSE responses?
cards.back: Disables Nginx's response buffering for this connection. Without it, Nginx (if used as a proxy) buffers the entire SSE stream before sending to the client — defeating the streaming behavior. The header tells the proxy to pass bytes through immediately.

cards.front: What is the Drizzle ORM `sql` tagged template, and when must you use it?
cards.back: `sql\`...\`` — creates a parameterized SQL expression that Drizzle executes safely. Must use it when writing raw SQL (e.g., `SET LOCAL`, `set_config()`). Drizzle cannot express PostgreSQL-specific operations like `SET LOCAL` through its query builder API.

cards.front: What Yjs data structure is used for a collaborative text document in Meridian?
cards.back: `Y.Doc` with `Y.Text` or `Y.XmlFragment` for text content. The Yjs provider (WebSocket) synchronizes the Y.Doc between clients. Lexical is connected to Yjs via a binding plugin that maps Lexical nodes to Yjs types.

cards.front: Why must secrets like JWT_SECRET and DATABASE_URL use environment variables, never hardcoded values?
cards.back: Hardcoded secrets are committed to version control, where they persist forever even after deletion. Environment variables keep secrets out of source code, allow per-environment values, and enable secret rotation without code changes. Meridian rule: always `process.env.X` or `import.meta.env.VITE_X`.

cards.front: What Zod method validates URL path parameters (e.g., `:workspaceId`), and why are they validated separately from the body?
cards.back: `z.string().uuid()` applied to `req.params.workspaceId`. Path params are strings — they come from the URL, not JSON body. Even trusted-looking params can be malformed or malicious. Validate params with Zod before using them in DB queries.

cards.front: What does the `memory_graph` table store, and what does the `weight` column represent?
cards.back: `memory_graph` stores directed connections between memories: `from_memory_id → to_memory_id`. The `weight` (FLOAT, 0.0–1.0) represents relationship strength. Higher weight = stronger semantic association. Used for multi-hop memory retrieval: retrieve a memory, then follow edges with weight > 0.6 to include related memories.

cards.front: What is the `block_versions` table for, and what triggers a new version entry?
cards.back: `block_versions` stores the full content history of each block. A new row is inserted whenever a block's content changes (after debounce/threshold). Columns: `block_id`, `content` (JSONB snapshot), `created_at`. Used for version history and undo features.

cards.front: What is the `writeMemory` UPSERT pattern, and why use UPSERT instead of INSERT?
cards.back: `INSERT INTO user_memory ... ON CONFLICT (user_id, workspace_id, memory_key) DO UPDATE SET memory_value = EXCLUDED.memory_value, embedding = EXCLUDED.embedding, last_accessed_at = NOW()`. UPSERT prevents duplicate memory keys for the same user/workspace — calling `writeMemory` with the same key updates the existing record rather than creating duplicates.

M01.title: M01 Quiz — System Design Before Code

questions.question: Which of the following is one of Meridian's three load-bearing constraints?
questions.answer: Browser-only collaborative editing state
questions.explanation: Yjs CRDT requires browser-only state. This constraint eliminates SSR frameworks like Next.js and forces a Vite SPA architecture.

questions.question: Why is Next.js incompatible with Meridian?
options: Next.js is slower than Vite for development
Server-side rendering conflicts with Yjs CRDT, which requires browser-only state
questions.answer: Server-side rendering conflicts with Yjs CRDT, which requires browser-only state
questions.explanation: Collaborative editing state in Yjs lives in the browser. SSR would attempt to render this state on the server where it cannot exist.

questions.question: What does an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) document?
options: A list of bugs found during code review
A technology choice with context, decision, rationale, and consequences
questions.answer: A technology choice with context, decision, rationale, and consequences
questions.explanation: An ADR captures why a choice was made (not just what was chosen) and what it commits the project to. It makes implicit decisions explicit and auditable.

questions.question: Name the three load-bearing constraints that determine Meridian's entire technology stack.
questions.answer_key: 1. Browser-only collaborative editor (Lexical + Yjs CRDT). 2. Below-app-layer tenant isolation (PostgreSQL RLS). 3. In-database vector storage (pgvector).
questions.rubric: 1 point each for correctly identifying and briefly explaining each constraint. Full credit requires all three.

questions.question: What is the 'Consequences' section of an ADR for, and why is it important?
questions.answer_key: The Consequences section describes what the technology choice commits the project to: what becomes easier, what becomes harder, and what is no longer possible. It is important because it forces the author to think beyond the immediate decision to its long-term effects.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains that Consequences documents long-term implications and commitments. Partial credit: mentions it describes trade-offs without the 'commitment' aspect.

questions.question: In Meridian's Docker Compose setup, what hostname does the server container use to connect to PostgreSQL?
questions.answer: postgres
questions.explanation: In Docker Compose, containers on the same network resolve each other by service name. The postgres service is named 'postgres', so the server uses that as the hostname.

questions.question: Which TypeScript compiler option prevents accessing array elements without a null check?
questions.answer: noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true
questions.explanation: noUncheckedIndexedAccess makes array[0] return T | undefined, forcing null checks on all indexed access. This catches a class of runtime errors that strict alone misses.

questions.question: What is the correct pnpm command to install all workspace dependencies?
questions.answer: pnpm install (from the root)
questions.explanation: Running pnpm install from the workspace root installs all packages and hoists shared dependencies. You never need to run install separately in each package folder.

questions.question: Why must secrets like JWT_SECRET never be hardcoded, and what is the correct pattern?
questions.answer_key: Hardcoded secrets are committed to version control and persist forever even after deletion. The correct pattern is process.env.JWT_SECRET on the server and import.meta.env.VITE_X on the client, loaded from a .env file that is in .gitignore.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains the persistence in version history AND gives the correct env var pattern for both server and client.

questions.question: What are the four services in Meridian's Docker Compose topology, and what depends on what?
questions.answer_key: postgres, redis, server, client. Server depends_on postgres and redis. Client depends_on server (for proxy forwarding). Postgres and redis have no dependencies.
questions.rubric: 1 point for listing all four services. 1 point for correct dependency order (server depends on postgres + redis).

questions.question: What does `AS RESTRICTIVE` mean in a PostgreSQL RLS policy?
options: The policy only applies to SELECT queries
The policy must be satisfied even when other permissive policies allow access
The policy applies to all roles including superusers
questions.answer: The policy must be satisfied even when other permissive policies allow access
questions.explanation: RESTRICTIVE policies form an AND condition with all other policies. No permissive policy can override a restrictive policy. This guarantees workspace isolation cannot be bypassed.

questions.question: What is the third parameter of `set_config('app.current_workspace_id', id, true)` for?
options: Enables encryption of the setting value
Makes the setting visible to all database users
Scopes the setting to the current transaction only
Sets the setting globally for all sessions
questions.answer: Scopes the setting to the current transaction only
questions.explanation: The third parameter `true` means `is_local = true`. The setting is transaction-scoped and automatically reset when the transaction ends. This prevents workspace context from leaking between requests in a connection pool.

questions.question: What pgvector index type does Meridian use for similarity search?
questions.answer: HNSW
questions.explanation: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) provides approximate nearest neighbor search with better query performance than IVFFlat. Created with: CREATE INDEX ON user_memory USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops);

questions.question: What `ON DELETE` behavior must the `audit_log` table use for `user_id` and `workspace_id` FKs?
questions.answer: SET NULL
questions.explanation: ON DELETE SET NULL preserves audit records when users or workspaces are deleted. Cascade would destroy the audit trail, which violates compliance requirements.

questions.question: Explain why the application DB user must have `rolbypassrls = false`. What would happen if it were true?
questions.answer_key: If rolbypassrls = true, all RLS policies are silently ignored for that role. Every query would return all rows regardless of workspace_id, completely defeating tenant isolation. The app user must have rolbypassrls = false so that RLS policies are enforced on every query.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains what BYPASSRLS does AND the tenant isolation consequence. Partial: states it bypasses RLS without explaining the consequence.

questions.question: What are the three memory tiers in Meridian, and what is different about each tier's lifecycle?
questions.answer_key: Session: cleared explicitly at end of conversation. Medium: evicted after 30 days of inactivity (last_accessed_at), LRU eviction. Deep: permanent, requires explicit promotion signal, never auto-evicted.
questions.rubric: 1 point per tier with correct lifecycle description. 3 points total.

questions.question: What bcrypt cost factor does Meridian require for password hashing?
questions.explanation: Cost factor 12 is the architecture minimum. Cost 10 (Node.js default) is too low for production. Cost 12 adds ~400ms per hash — expensive for an attacker, acceptable for a login.

questions.question: What JWT claim carries the user ID in Meridian?
questions.answer: sub
questions.explanation: The `sub` (subject) claim is the standard JWT claim for identifying the subject of the token. In Meridian, it contains the user's UUID as a string.

questions.question: Why must the login handler run a dummy bcrypt comparison when the user email is not found?
options: To prevent timing attacks that could enumerate valid email addresses
To comply with GDPR email privacy rules
To warm up the bcrypt cache
questions.answer: To prevent timing attacks that could enumerate valid email addresses
questions.explanation: Without a dummy hash, 'user not found' returns instantly while 'wrong password' takes 400ms. This timing difference allows an attacker to determine which emails are registered.

questions.question: What does Meridian store in Redis for refresh tokens — the raw token or a hash?
options: The raw token, for fast comparison
A SHA-256 hash of the token
A bcrypt hash of the token
questions.answer: A SHA-256 hash of the token
questions.explanation: Storing the raw token means a Redis breach reveals all active sessions. SHA-256 hashes are stored; the client holds the raw token. If Redis is compromised, the attacker gets useless hashes.

questions.question: Describe what happens in a refresh token reuse attack and how Meridian detects it.
questions.answer_key: An attacker steals a refresh token and uses it after the legitimate user has already rotated it. Meridian detects this because the Redis key (rt:{userId}) is already gone when the attacker's request arrives — the legitimate user already deleted it during rotation. The server sees a missing Redis key as a reuse signal and revokes all active tokens for the user.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains stolen token scenario, Redis key already missing, and revoke-all response. Partial: understands reuse detection but misses the revoke-all consequence.

questions.question: What is the correct Express middleware pipeline order for Meridian?
options: JSON → auth → Helmet → CORS → rateLimit → Zod → handler
Helmet → CORS → rateLimit → JSON → auth → workspace → Zod → handler
auth → Helmet → JSON → rateLimit → workspace → Zod → handler
Zod → auth → workspace → Helmet → JSON → handler
questions.answer: Helmet → CORS → rateLimit → JSON → auth → workspace → Zod → handler
questions.explanation: Security headers (Helmet) must be first. Rate limiting before body parsing (prevents large body DoS). Auth before workspace (workspace check requires knowing who the user is). Zod immediately before handler logic.

questions.question: What does `safeParse` return when validation fails?
questions.answer: { success: false, error: ZodError }
questions.explanation: safeParse never throws. It returns { success: true, data } on success and { success: false, error } on failure. The error is a ZodError with structured issue details.

questions.question: Where must the `workspace_id` come from in the tenant middleware — never from where?
options: From req.params or verified middleware — never from req.body
From the JWT claims — never from anywhere else
questions.answer: From req.params or verified middleware — never from req.body
questions.explanation: A client can put any workspaceId in req.body. Trusting it allows users to scope requests to workspaces they don't belong to. req.params is controlled by route patterns and verified by membership checks.

questions.question: What is the four-parameter Express error handler signature, and what happens inside it for a ZodError vs a generic error?
questions.answer_key: (err, req, res, next) => void. For a ZodError: return res.status(400).json({ errors: err.errors }). For AuthenticationError: return res.status(401). For AuthorizationError: return res.status(403). For all other errors: log the error, return res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal server error' }).
questions.rubric: Full credit: correct four-parameter signature, 400 for Zod, correct status for at least two other error types, 500 fallback.

questions.question: Why is `SET LOCAL` required instead of `SET` in tenant middleware, and what goes wrong with `SET`?
questions.answer_key: SET is session-level: the setting persists for the entire lifetime of the database connection. In a connection pool, connections are reused between requests. Using SET means the next request that reuses the connection inherits the previous request's workspace_id, causing cross-tenant context leakage. SET LOCAL is transaction-scoped and automatically resets when the transaction ends.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains connection pool reuse AND transaction scope of SET LOCAL. Partial: mentions one but not both.

questions.question: What does Vite's `server.proxy` do in Meridian's development setup?
options: Forwards /api/* requests from the Vite dev server to the Express backend
Syncs environment variables between client and server
questions.answer: Forwards /api/* requests from the Vite dev server to the Express backend
questions.explanation: The proxy allows the browser to make /api/* requests to Vite (same origin, no CORS), which Vite forwards to Express. This avoids CORS configuration during development.

questions.question: What is the React 18 rule about state updates?
options: State updates must be immutable — never mutate objects in place
State must be stored in Redux
State updates are automatically batched into a single render
questions.answer: State updates must be immutable — never mutate objects in place
questions.explanation: React 18 concurrent mode requires immutable state. Mutations to existing objects won't trigger re-renders. Always create new objects/arrays.

questions.question: What must `RequireAuth` render while the auth check is still loading?
options: null or a spinner — not a redirect
questions.answer: null or a spinner — not a redirect
questions.explanation: If RequireAuth redirects to login while isLoading is true, authenticated users briefly see the login page and are then redirected back. Rendering null during loading prevents this flash.

questions.question: How do you read Zustand store state outside a React component without staleness issues?
options: useStore() called at the top of the file
questions.answer: useAuthStore.getState()
questions.explanation: getState() directly accesses the current store state without subscribing. The hook useAuthStore() only works inside React components and is subject to closure staleness in callbacks.

questions.question: Describe the three-tier React Router structure in Meridian and what each tier protects.
questions.answer_key: Tier 1 — Public routes (/login, /register): no auth required, accessible to unauthenticated users. Tier 2 — Auth guard (RequireAuth): wraps all authenticated routes, redirects to login if user is null. Tier 3 — Workspace routes (/workspace/:id/*): nested inside auth guard, WorkspaceShell loads workspace context, server middleware verifies workspace membership.
questions.rubric: 1 point per tier with correct description of what it protects. 3 points total.

questions.question: What is Lexical's EditorState?
options: A mutable DOM representation of editor content
An immutable snapshot of all editor content at a point in time
A Redux reducer for editor actions
The HTML string of the editor's current content
questions.answer: An immutable snapshot of all editor content at a point in time
questions.explanation: EditorState is immutable. Every change produces a new EditorState. You read it with editor.getEditorState().read(() => {...}) and mutate via editor.update(() => {...}).

questions.question: How do you programmatically modify Lexical editor content?
questions.answer: editor.update(() => { /* mutations here */ })
questions.explanation: Mutations must be inside editor.update(). Lexical creates a new EditorState from the mutation. Mutations outside update() will throw or be silently ignored.

questions.question: What are the two data representations Meridian uses for page content?
options: Yjs Y.Doc (live CRDT) and blocks table (persistent PostgreSQL records)
questions.answer: Yjs Y.Doc (live CRDT) and blocks table (persistent PostgreSQL records)
questions.explanation: The Y.Doc is the live collaborative editing state in memory. The blocks table is the durable, queryable record in PostgreSQL. syncPageBlocks() reconciles them.

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the `block_versions` table and what triggers a new version entry.
questions.answer_key: block_versions stores the full content history of each block as JSONB snapshots. A new row is inserted when a block's content changes (after a debounce threshold to avoid storing every keystroke). It enables version history, undo, and content auditing.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains content history purpose, JSONB snapshot, debounce pattern. Partial: explains history but not the debounce mechanism.

questions.question: What role does Yjs play in the Meridian editor, and how does it relate to Lexical?
questions.answer_key: Yjs is a CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) library. The Y.Doc is the shared collaborative document state. It is connected to Lexical via a Yjs binding plugin that maps Lexical nodes to Yjs types. The WebSocket provider synchronizes the Y.Doc between all connected clients in real-time.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains CRDT, Y.Doc as shared state, Lexical binding, WebSocket sync. Partial: explains sync without the binding mechanism.

questions.question: What two fields must ALWAYS appear in a Meridian vector search WHERE clause?
questions.answer: user_id and workspace_id
questions.explanation: Both user_id and workspace_id must be present. Omitting user_id could return other users' memories. Omitting workspace_id could return memories from other workspaces. Both scopes are required.

questions.question: What HTTP mechanism does Meridian use for AI streaming responses?
questions.answer: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
questions.explanation: SSE is used for AI streaming because it is server-to-client only (unidirectional), uses plain HTTP, and has native browser reconnect logic. WebSocket is bidirectional and used for Yjs document sync.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `LLMProvider` interface?
options: To cache AI responses for performance
To allow switching AI providers without changing route handlers
To rate limit AI API calls
questions.answer: To allow switching AI providers without changing route handlers
questions.explanation: The LLMProvider abstraction decouples route handlers from specific providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Ollama). The LLMRouter selects a provider at runtime. Changing providers requires no handler changes.

questions.question: What distance threshold does Meridian use to filter pgvector similarity results?
questions.explanation: distance < 0.3 (cosine distance) means only memories with ≥70% semantic similarity are included. Higher threshold returns more results with lower relevance.

questions.question: Describe the complete `writeMemory` UPSERT pattern and explain why UPSERT is used instead of INSERT.
questions.answer_key: INSERT INTO user_memory (user_id, workspace_id, memory_key, memory_value, embedding, tier) VALUES (...) ON CONFLICT (user_id, workspace_id, memory_key) DO UPDATE SET memory_value = EXCLUDED.memory_value, embedding = EXCLUDED.embedding, last_accessed_at = NOW(). UPSERT is used because calling writeMemory with the same key should update the existing record, not create a duplicate. INSERT alone would fail or create multiple records for the same memory key.
questions.rubric: Full credit: includes ON CONFLICT clause, updates all three fields (value, embedding, last_accessed_at), explains duplicate prevention.

questions.question: What does `X-Accel-Buffering: no` do, and why is it needed for SSE?
questions.answer_key: It disables Nginx's response buffering for this connection. Without it, Nginx buffers the full SSE stream before sending it to the client, defeating the streaming behavior — all tokens would arrive at once instead of incrementally. The header tells the proxy to pass bytes through immediately as they are written.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains Nginx buffering AND the consequence (all tokens arrive at once). Partial: explains it disables buffering without the consequence.

questions.question: What SQL query verifies which tables have RLS enabled?
options: SELECT tablename, rowsecurity FROM pg_tables WHERE schemaname = 'public';
questions.answer: SELECT tablename, rowsecurity FROM pg_tables WHERE schemaname = 'public';
questions.explanation: pg_tables contains a rowsecurity boolean for each table. Workspace-scoped tables must have rowsecurity = true.

questions.question: Where must user-provided text go in an LLM API call to prevent prompt injection?
options: Concatenated into the system prompt string
questions.answer: In the messages[].content array only
questions.explanation: The LLM API structurally separates system (instructions) from messages (user content). User input in the system parameter gives users control over the instructions. User input must only go in messages[].

questions.question: When must `logAuditEvent` be called relative to the AI provider call?
options: After the AI call completes successfully
After the AI call in a finally block
questions.answer: Before the AI provider call
questions.explanation: If the provider call fails, the audit log must still have a record. Logging after means failures leave no trace. Log before, always.

questions.question: What is the adversarial RLS test?
options: Attempting SQL injection against the login endpoint
Setting workspace context to B, then querying workspace A's data and expecting 0 rows
Verifying that superusers cannot log in
Testing that the app user cannot create tables
questions.answer: Setting workspace context to B, then querying workspace A's data and expecting 0 rows
questions.explanation: This test proves RLS is actually enforced at runtime. Setting context to workspace B and querying workspace A's data should return zero rows. If rows are returned, RLS is disabled or misconfigured.

questions.question: Name two techniques for protecting against prompt injection via memory values injected into the system prompt.
questions.answer_key: 1. Sanitization: strip phrases like 'ignore previous instructions', 'you are now', 'act as' from memory values before injection. 2. XML framing: wrap injected memory in <user_context> tags to signal to the model that the content is data, not instructions. 3. Hard length limits on injected memory values (e.g., 500 chars) to limit injection payload size.
questions.rubric: 2 points: any two of the three techniques correctly described. 1 point: one technique described.

questions.question: What six AI action types must be logged to audit_log?
questions.answer_key: ai.complete, ai.stream, ai.memory.write, ai.memory.retrieve, ai.memory.promote, ai.memory.evict
questions.rubric: 1 point for each correct action type. 6 points total. Accept minor variations in naming as long as intent is clear.

questions.question: Which gap is classified as P0 (fix immediately, before next commit)?
options: Missing Zod validation on a POST route
Missing workspace_id in a DB query
Audit log placed after AI provider call
questions.answer: Missing workspace_id in a DB query
questions.explanation: Missing workspace_id is P0 because it can cause cross-tenant data leakage immediately. This is the highest blast radius gap and must be fixed before any other commit.

questions.question: What is the correct gap report priority for 'audit log placed after provider call'?
questions.answer: P1
questions.explanation: P1 = fix before any user data enters the system. Missing audit logs mean AI actions on user data are unaccountable. Critical but not the same blast radius as cross-tenant leakage (P0).

questions.question: What grep pattern verifies bcrypt cost factor is correct across the codebase?
options: grep -rn 'bcrypt\.hash(' server/src — then verify second argument is 12
questions.answer: grep -rn 'bcrypt\.hash(' server/src — then verify second argument is 12
questions.explanation: grep finds all bcrypt.hash() calls. Each result must be manually inspected to confirm the second argument is 12, not a variable that could be any value.

questions.question: The Five-Question Framework produces a binary result for each question. What does 'binary' mean here?
options: The output is encoded in binary format
Each question has exactly two sub-questions
Each question has a pass or fail answer — no gradient
The framework runs in two phases
questions.answer: Each question has a pass or fail answer — no gradient
questions.explanation: Non-negotiable rules are binary: your code either follows them or it doesn't. There is no 'mostly compliant'. A single missing workspace_id is a fail for Q1.

questions.question: List the six conditions that define 'production-ready' for a Meridian implementation.
questions.answer_key: 1. All five framework questions return zero violations. 2. audit_log has entries for every AI action in the test suite. 3. Adversarial RLS test passes. 4. All bcrypt hashes use cost 12. 5. Refresh token handler has reuse detection branch. 6. No user-provided text in any system prompt string.

questions.question: Describe the complete gap remediation workflow: from identifying a gap to confirming it is resolved.
questions.answer_key: 1. Run the Five-Question Framework grep patterns against the codebase. 2. Review each grep result manually. 3. Write a structured gap report entry (gap ID, question, file, line, description, risk, fix). 4. Prioritize by P0/P1/P2. 5. Apply one fix per commit with a structured commit message referencing the gap ID. 6. Re-run the specific grep pattern for that question to confirm the violation no longer appears. 7. Mark the gap as resolved in the gap report.
questions.rubric: Full credit: identifies all seven steps or equivalent. Partial: includes grep, report, prioritize, fix, re-run — 5 of 7 steps earns partial credit.

M01.title: M01 Mastery Check — System Design Before Code
M01.instructions: Complete both tasks. You pass this module when you can produce both outputs from memory without referencing lesson material.

tasks.title: Derive the Stack from Constraints
tasks.prompt: Given only these three requirements: (1) real-time collaborative editing in the browser, (2) enterprise multi-tenancy enforced below the application layer, (3) semantic search without an external service — derive the Meridian technology choices that each constraint forces. For each constraint, name the constraint, the forced technology, and at least one alternative it eliminates.
success_criteria: Constraint 1 → Lexical + Yjs CRDT → eliminates Next.js/SSR frameworks
Constraint 2 → PostgreSQL RLS → eliminates application-layer multi-tenancy approaches
Constraint 3 → pgvector extension → eliminates Pinecone, Weaviate, external vector DBs

tasks.title: Write an ADR
tasks.prompt: Write a complete Architecture Decision Record for Meridian's choice of Drizzle ORM over Prisma. Your ADR must include: Context, Decision, Rationale (with at least two reasons), and Consequences (what it commits you to).
success_criteria: Context section explains the ORM selection problem
Decision clearly states Drizzle is chosen
Rationale includes TypeScript-first design and no code generation step
Consequences section mentions what trade-offs are accepted (e.g., less ecosystem tooling than Prisma)

tasks.title: Write the pnpm-workspace.yaml and root tsconfig.json
tasks.prompt: From memory, write the pnpm-workspace.yaml file and the root tsconfig.json for Meridian. The workspace has three packages: client, server, db. The tsconfig must include all three non-negotiable TypeScript options.
success_criteria: pnpm-workspace.yaml lists packages: ['client', 'server', 'db']
tsconfig.json includes strict: true
tsconfig.json includes noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true
tsconfig.json includes exactOptionalPropertyTypes: true

tasks.title: Identify and Fix Docker Compose Hostname Bug
tasks.prompt: A colleague shares this .env for the server container: DATABASE_URL=postgresql://meridian:pass@localhost:5432/meridian. They say the server container cannot connect to PostgreSQL. Diagnose the problem and write the corrected DATABASE_URL.
success_criteria: Correctly identifies 'localhost' as the bug
Explains that containers on the same Docker network resolve by service name
Corrects to: postgresql://meridian:pass@postgres:5432/meridian

tasks.title: Write the complete RLS policy for the pages table
tasks.prompt: From memory, write the SQL to: (1) enable RLS on the pages table, (2) create a RESTRICTIVE workspace isolation policy, (3) create the trigger function that the policy uses. Do not reference the lesson — reproduce the exact pattern.
success_criteria: ALTER TABLE pages ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY
Policy uses AS RESTRICTIVE
Policy USING clause: workspace_id = current_setting('app.current_workspace_id')::uuid
No use of superuser-level SET — the policy uses current_setting()

tasks.title: Debug the Memory Query
tasks.prompt:
Review this vector search query and identify every violation of Meridian's architecture rules:

```sql
SELECT memory_key, memory_value
FROM user_memory
WHERE user_id = $1
ORDER BY embedding <-> $2 LIMIT 10;
```
success_criteria: Identifies missing workspace_id = $N in WHERE clause
Identifies wrong operator: <-> (L2 distance) should be <=> (cosine distance)
Identifies no distance threshold filter (should add: AND distance < 0.3 using a subquery or CTE)

tasks.title: Find All Auth Implementation Gaps
success_criteria: Missing dummy bcrypt hash when user not found (timing attack vulnerability)
JWT payload uses 'userId' — should use 'sub' claim
JWT expiry is '7d' — should be '15m' for access tokens
No Zod validation on req.body
No refresh token issued — only access token

tasks.title: Write the Refresh Token Rotation Handler
tasks.prompt: Write a TypeScript Express route handler for POST /auth/refresh. It must: validate the incoming refresh token, detect token reuse (Redis key already gone), rotate the token, and return a new access token. Include the reuse detection branch that revokes all tokens.
success_criteria: Reads Redis key rt:{userId}
If key is missing: revokeAllTokensForUser and return 401
Computes SHA-256 of incoming token and compares to stored hash
Deletes old Redis key immediately
Generates new access token (15m) and new refresh token
Stores new refresh token hash in Redis

tasks.title: Write the Express App Skeleton
tasks.prompt: From memory, write the Meridian Express app.ts skeleton showing the complete middleware pipeline in correct order. Include at least: Helmet, CORS, rateLimit, JSON body parser, authMiddleware, and errorHandler registration. Use TypeScript.
success_criteria: app.use(helmet()) is first
app.use(cors()) before rateLimit and JSON parser
app.use(rateLimit) before JSON body parser
app.use(express.json()) before authMiddleware
app.use(authMiddleware) before route registration
app.use(errorHandler) is last — four parameter signature

tasks.title: Find the Tenant Middleware Bug
success_criteria: workspace_id from req.body — must be from req.params
SET not SET LOCAL — will cause workspace context to leak in connection pools

tasks.title: Write the RequireAuth component
tasks.prompt: Write the Meridian RequireAuth component in TypeScript React. It must handle all three authentication states correctly. Use Zustand's useAuthStore.
success_criteria: If isLoading === true: return null (not a redirect)
If user !== null: return <Outlet />
If user === null: return <Navigate to='/login' replace />

tasks.title: Write the Auth Store
tasks.prompt: Write a Zustand auth store (useAuthStore) for Meridian. It must include: user state (User | null), isLoading state, setUser action, and a checkSession async action that calls GET /api/auth/me and handles all three outcomes (loading, found, not found).
success_criteria: isLoading: true initial state during session check
setIsLoading(false) called in both success and error paths
user set to null on 401 or error
checkSession sets isLoading to false before returning in all branches

M07.title: M07 Mastery Check — The Editor Layer

tasks.title: Find the Lexical State Bug
tasks.prompt:
This code attempts to read the editor's text content. Find the bug:

```typescript
const text = editor.getEditorState().root.children[0].text
```
success_criteria: Identifies direct property access on EditorState is wrong
Correct pattern: editor.getEditorState().read(() => { const root = $getRoot(); const text = root.getTextContent(); return text })
Explains that EditorState is opaque — content must be accessed inside a read() callback

tasks.title: Describe the Two-Representation Model
tasks.prompt: Explain Meridian's two-representation model for page content: what each representation is, when each is the source of truth, and how they are kept in sync. No code required — written explanation only.
success_criteria: Identifies Yjs Y.Doc as live collaborative state (source of truth while editing)
Identifies blocks table as durable persistent record (source of truth at rest)
Describes syncPageBlocks() or equivalent sync mechanism
Notes that Lexical is connected to Yjs via a binding

M08.title: M08 Mastery Check — AI Layer Integration

tasks.title: Write the LLMProvider Interface
tasks.prompt: Write the TypeScript LLMProvider interface and AnthropicProvider implementation skeleton. The interface must have complete and stream methods with correct TypeScript types. The AnthropicProvider must call logAuditEvent before making any API call.
success_criteria: Interface has complete(req: LLMRequest): Promise<string>
Interface has stream(req: LLMRequest): AsyncIterable<string>
AnthropicProvider implements both methods
logAuditEvent called before Anthropic API call in both methods

tasks.title: Fix the Memory Retrieval Query
success_criteria: Missing workspace_id in WHERE clause — must add AND workspace_id = {workspaceId}
Missing distance threshold — should add AND embedding <=> ... < 0.3 or filter results
Function signature must accept workspaceId parameter

tasks.title: Write the Adversarial RLS Test
tasks.prompt: Write a complete Vitest test that proves RLS workspace isolation works. The test must: set workspace context to workspace B, attempt to query workspace A's pages, and assert 0 rows are returned. Include a second test that proves workspace B's own pages ARE visible.
success_criteria: Uses SET LOCAL or set_config to set context to workspace B
Queries pages WHERE workspace_id = workspaceA UUID
Asserts rows.length === 0
Second test sets context to workspace B, queries workspace B's pages, asserts length > 0

tasks.title: Write the sanitizeMemoryValue Function
tasks.prompt: Write the TypeScript sanitizeMemoryValue function. It must: remove common injection phrases, enforce a maximum length of 500 characters, and be applied to all memory values before they are injected into a system prompt.
success_criteria: Removes 'ignore.*instructions' pattern (case insensitive)
Removes 'you are now' pattern
Removes 'act as' pattern
.slice(0, 500) hard limit applied last
Returns sanitized string

M10.title: M10 Mastery Check — Code Efficacy Assessment

tasks.title: Run the Five-Question Framework on a Code Sample
success_criteria: Q1: PASS — workspace_id comes from req.workspace.id (via verified middleware)
Q2: FAIL — no Zod safeParse on req.body before handler logic (P2)
Q3: FAIL — logAuditEvent is called AFTER the AI provider call (P1)
Q4: N/A — no bcrypt in this handler
Q5: N/A — no refresh token in this handler

tasks.title: Write a Gap Report Entry
tasks.prompt: Write a structured gap report entry for the Q3 violation found in MC10-T1. Include: Gap ID, Question number, File (use a placeholder), Line number (estimate), Description, Risk, and the specific code fix.
success_criteria: Gap ID assigned (e.g., GAP-001)
Q3 identified
Description explains audit log called after provider, not before
Risk: AI actions not logged if provider throws
Fix: move logAuditEvent to before llmRouter.complete()
Priority P1 assigned

paths.label: Full Rebuild Playbook
paths.description: All 27 lessons in module order. Covers every phase of Meridian from constraints to production readiness.

paths.label: Fast-Track Rebuild
paths.description: Skip the conceptual framing. Focus on implementation lessons. For learners who already know why and need the how.

paths.label: Security-First Track
paths.description: Covers the architecture decisions that matter most from a security standpoint: schema/RLS, auth, server hardening, AI safety, and efficacy.
